questions.question: Which one of these is the formal definition of `f(n) = O(g(n))`?
options: ∃ c, n₀ > 0 such that for all n ≥ n₀, f(n) ≤ c · g(n)
f and g have the same leading term
questions.explanation: Big-O is defined by 'eventually bounded by a constant multiple.' The two existence quantifiers (∃ c) and (∃ n₀) — and the universal (∀ n ≥ n₀) — capture 'up to a constant, for large enough n.' This single definition implies all the algebraic rules.

questions.question: Is `3n² + 5n + 7` in Θ(n²)?
options: No — the constant 3 is in the way
Yes — lower-order terms and constants are dropped by Θ
questions.explanation: Θ-notation discards constants and lower-order terms. (3n²+5n+7)/n² → 3 (a positive constant) as n → ∞. So the polynomial is Θ(n²). The polynomial is also O(n²), Ω(n²), and o(n^2.5).

questions.question: Why is the base of the logarithm irrelevant inside O(log n)?
options: Change of base: log_a(n) = log_b(n) / log_b(a) — the ratio is a positive constant — so O(log_a n) and O(log_b n) describe the same set.
It's a convention without mathematical basis
questions.explanation: log_a(n) = log_b(n) · (1 / log_b(a)). For constant a, b > 1 the factor 1/log_b(a) is just a positive constant — and Big-O absorbs constants. So inside O(...), all logs are equivalent. The same is NOT true for 2^n vs 3^n: those differ by a function, not a constant.

questions.question: An algorithm A runs in 1000n nanoseconds; algorithm B runs in 0.01n² nanoseconds. For which n does A become faster?
options: A is always faster — it's O(n)
When 1000n < 0.01n² → n > 100,000. Before that, B is faster despite being O(n²).
questions.explanation: Big-O describes the limit, not pre-asymptotic behavior. With c_A = 1000 and c_B = 0.01, B is faster until n ≈ 100,000. This is why algorithm analysis must include constants: 'better asymptotic' doesn't mean 'better for production input sizes.'

questions.question: Which is correct?
options: Neither — O(n) and O(n²) are disjoint
questions.explanation: Every function bounded by c·n is also bounded by c·n² (for sufficiently large n). So O(n) is a *subset* of O(n²). The reverse is false: n² is in O(n²) but not in O(n). Big-O ⊆ relations encode dominance: faster-growth bounds are bigger sets.

questions.question: Show: `log(n!) = Θ(n log n)`.
options: True — by Stirling's approximation, n! ≈ √(2πn)·(n/e)ⁿ, so log(n!) ≈ n log n - n + O(log n) which is Θ(n log n).
questions.explanation: Stirling: log(n!) ≈ n log n - n + (1/2) log(2πn). The dominant term is n log n; everything else is lower order. So log(n!) = Θ(n log n). This is the basis of the comparison-sort lower bound: any sort that compares pairs must make Ω(log(n!)) = Ω(n log n) comparisons.
